coredata.dc:title: Chlorine-functionalized carbon dots for highly efficient photodegradation of pollutants under visible-light irradiation
coredata.prism:copyright: Copyright © 2015 Elsevier B.V. All rights reserved.
coredata.dc:creator: Hu, Shengliang
Ding, Yanli
Chang, Qing
Yang, Jinlong
Lin, Kui
coredata.dc:description: Chlorine-functionalized carbon dots (Cl-CDs) were prepared by the substitution reaction between Cl radicals into thionyl chloride molecules and carbon dots with containing OH/COOH groups at their surface (O-CDs). The obtained Cl-CDs with a size of 2–5nm contain 2–3% Cl atoms and emit blue light. Compared with amine-functionalzed carbon dots (N-CDs) and O-CDs, Cl-CDs exhibit much higher photocatalytic activity under visible-light irradiation. The thermally and chemically stable phthalocyanine can be even degraded quickly through Cl-CDs. This work suggests that surface engineering of carbon dots with heteroatoms can be used to enhance their photochemical properties.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon materials
Nanoparticles
Surfaces
Photocatalytic activity

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) TEM image of Cl-CDs; (b) Cl 2p XPS of Cl-CDs; (c) PL emission of Cl-CDs and the mixed solution of Cl-CDs and ZnPc excited at 400nm.

(a) UV–vis absorption spectra: Cl-CDs, ZnPc, Cl-CDs+ZnPc and the sample after visible-light irradiation of Cl-CDs+ZnPc; (b) a plot of the extent of photodegradation of ZnPc and CuPc vs. time for Cl-CDs.

(a) Photos of the mixed solution of Cl-CDs and ZnPc stood for 0 and 10 days, respectively; (b) photos of ZnPc solution stood for 0 and 10 days, respectively; (c) PL intensity change of the samples (containing the same concentration of Cl-CDs) excited at 400nm with the standing time; (d) the PL intensity changes of the samples (containing the same concentration of ZnPc) excited at 600nm with the standing time.
Photocatalytic degradation of ZnPc under visible light irradiation (λ >420nm), using Cl-CDs, O-CDs and N-CDs, respectively.

head.ce:title: Chlorine-functionalized carbon dots for highly efficient photodegradation of pollutants under visible-light irradiation

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Material Science and Engineering, North University of China, Taiyuan 030051, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Material Science and Engineering, North University of China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory of New Ceramics and Fine Processing, Tsinghua University, Beijing 100084, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of New Ceramics and Fine Processing, Tsinghua University

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Analytical Instrumentation Center, Tianjin University, Tianjin 300072, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Chlorine-functionalized carbon dots (Cl-CDs) were synthesized through the substitution reaction. Cl-CDs show highly photocatalytic activity under visible-light irradiation, and can quickly degrade phthalocyanine with the thermal and chemical stability. This work suggests that surface engineering of carbon dots with heteroatoms can be used to enhance their photochemical properties.

Chlorine-functionalized carbon dots (Cl-CDs) were prepared by the substitution reaction between Cl radicals into thionyl chloride molecules and carbon dots with containing OH/COOH groups at their surface (O-CDs). The obtained Cl-CDs with a size of 2–5nm contain 2–3% Cl atoms and emit blue light. Compared with amine-functionalzed carbon dots (N-CDs) and O-CDs, Cl-CDs exhibit much higher photocatalytic activity under visible-light irradiation. The thermally and chemically stable phthalocyanine can be even degraded quickly through Cl-CDs. This work suggests that surface engineering of carbon dots with heteroatoms can be used to enhance their photochemical properties.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon dots (CDs) exhibit unique and tunable photoluminescence behaviors, exceptional physicochemical properties without incurring the burden of intrinsic toxicity or elemental scarcity [1]. On the other hand, they can be synthesized inexpensively and easily by using various carbon sources, such as soot, fruit, grass and organic molecules [1–3]. Therefore, CDs are inspiring intensive research on their potential applications in many fields [4]. With industrialization and population growth, the environmental contamination caused by organic pollutant is becoming an overwhelming problem all over the world. To remove them from various waters and wastewaters, photocatalysis has emerged as one of the most promising technologies because it represents an easy way to utilize the energy of either natural sunlight or artificial indoor illumination, and is thus abundantly available everywhere in the world [5]. Although the recent studies have indicated that CDs can be employed to harvest visible light and convert it to shorter wavelength light through up-conversion, they only act as one component of the composites to enhance photocatalytic activity of the other component such as TiO2, Cu2O, Ag3PO4, Au [4,6]. Thus, the green and low cost CDs do not play a key role in the use of sunlight to degrade organic pollutants.

According to previous researches, engineering CDs with heteroatoms or surface groups may control the photoinduced charge behaviors [2,7,8]. In the present work, we design chlorine atoms (Cl) on the surface of CDs as ideal active sites for photocatalytic reaction to proceed. The Cl-functionalized CDs (Cl-CDs) show highly efficient photocatalytic performance. For example, they not only can degrade the common dyes (such as methylene blue, rhodamine-B) [2], but also can oxidize phthalocyanine (Pc) into CO2, water by using molecular O2 as oxidant under visible-light irradiation. Pc has a two-dimensional 18-п-electron aromatic system. Specially, it is a thermally, chemically and photo-chemically stable compound [9]. This leads to high difficulty in degrading Pc molecules through general photocatalysts, e.g. TiO2.

Cl-CDs were obtained by two steps. CDs that contained OH and/or COOH O-contained groups at their surface (named O-CDs) were first synthesized by thermal decomposition of organic molecules such as citrate, ethylene glycol, which had been reported widely [7]. Then surface chlorination of O-CDs was accomplished through the substitution reaction between Cl into thionyl chloride (SOCl2) molecules and OH/COOH radicals. Detailed experimental procedures were given in the Supplementary section.

Fig. 1(a) shows a transmission electron microscopy (TEM) image of Cl-CDs. The uniformly dispersed nanoparticles are observed and their sizes mainly range from 2 to 5nm. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was used to analyze element compositions of Cl-CDs (Fig. S1). The measured result indicates that Cl-CDs contain 2–3% of Cl atoms and 10–12% of O atoms besides carbon atoms. High-resolution XPS scans for Cl 2p peaks of Cl-CDs is shown in Fig. 1(b). The bonding energy peak of Cl 2p is centered on about 200eV, suggesting the covalent bonding of the chlorine atoms to the carbon skeleton [10]. This result is confirmed by the infrared (IR) spectra (Fig. S2).

Zinc Pc (ZnPc) is used for photodegradation studies to show photocatalytic activity of Cl-CDs. Due to water-insolubility of ZnPc, Cl-CDs are dispersed in the mixed solution of water and N,N-dimethylmethanamide (DMF). Fig. 1(c) shows photoluminescence (PL) spectra of Cl-CDs and the blend of Cl-CDs and ZnPc (Cl-CDs+ZnPc) in the mixed solution of DMF and H2O, respectively. It can be seen that the addition of ZnPc results in PL reduction. However, the corresponding UV–vis absorption intensity increases in contrast with Cl-CDs or ZnPc alone (Fig. 2a). After visible-light irradiation from a 300W xenon lamp, the sample absorption significantly decreases in the 600–800nm region. The temporal evolution of ZnPc concentration during photocatalytic degradation is shown in Fig. 2(b). ZnPc was degraded quickly with complete removal occurring after only 90s of reaction. To compare, ZnPc were displaced by copper Pc (CuPc) to investigate photocatalytic activity of Cl-CDs at the same conditions. As shown in Fig. 2b, CuPc was also degraded quickly similar to ZnPc through Cl-CDs, indicating their powerful capability in photodegradation of dyes.

In order to further prove highly efficient photodegradation performance of Cl-CDs, the blend solution of Cl-CDs and ZnPc was placed in the experimental room (low-light environments) without xenon lamp irradiation. As shown in Fig. 3(a), the sample color is transformed into yellow from green after 10 days. In contrast, ZnPc solution does not change color at the same past time (Fig. 3b). The mixed solution of Cl-CDs and ZnPc can emit blue and red light through different wavelength excitation. The blue light emission obtained at 400nm excitation is attributed to Cl-CDs (Fig. S3) while the red light emission obtained at 600nm excitation originates from ZnPc molecules (Fig. S4). Fig. 3(c) shows the PL intensity changes of Cl-CDs and the mixture with the standing time. The PL intensity of Cl-CDs decreases with the standing time while that of the mixture increases. After standing for 10 days, the blue light emission of both samples becomes stable. Similarly, Fig. 3(d) gives the PL intensity changes of ZnPc and the mixture with the standing time. The little effect of the red light emission from ZnPc solution on the standing time is observed. However, the red light emission of the mixed solution decreases significantly in several days. This implies that ZnPc molecules have been degraded by Cl-CDs under the weak visible-light irradiation.

By contrast, we performed the similar photodegradation experiments through blending amine-functionalized CDs (N-CDs) and O-CDs with ZnPc in mixed solution of DMF and H2O, respectively (Fig. 4 ). The degraded rate of N-CDs and O-CDs to ZnPc is much slower than that of Cl-CDs. Therefore, highly photocatalytic activity of Cl-CDs could be attributed to the chlorine-contained groups at the surface.

For CDs with a very small size, most of carbon atoms are exposed on the surface, thus their space charge distribution will depend on surface state. In the space charge region, the electric potential (V) of a point (x, y, z) can be described as [11]
(1)
∂ 2
V ( x , y , z )
∂
x 2
+
∂ 2
V ( x , y , z )
∂
y 2
+
∂ 2
V ( x , y , z )
∂
z 2
= −
ρ
ε r
ε 0
where ɛ
r , ɛ 0 and ρ are the relative dielectric constant, the vacuum permittivity and the space charge density, respectively. For simplicity, the space charge distribution was considered as one dimension. Then Eq. (1) can be written as
(2)
d 2
V ( x )
d
x 2
= −
ρ
ε r
ε 0

To solve Eq. (2), V can be obtained as,
(3)
V ( x ) = −
ρ
2
ε r
ε 0
x 2

It can be seen that V is a function of the x coordinates and is affected by ρ that is connected with the kinds and quantities of surficial heteroatoms. Therefore, the difference of V is created in a nanoparticle when Cl atoms are introduced at the surface of O-CDs. The changes of V with Cl and O-contained positions could facilitate electron–hole pair separation and induce faster carrier migration. Thus, the more excited-electron and hole are produced. As shown in Scheme 1 , they will attack O2 and OH− (and/or H2O) to generate more active hydroxyl radicals (·OH). Since the ·OH radicals play a key role in oxidizing dyes into CO2 and water shown in Eq. (4), Cl-CDs exhibit higher efficiency than O-CDs for photodegradation of Pc.
(4)
Pc + ⋅ OH → PcOH ⋅
⟶
O 2
/
H 2
O
CO 2
+
H 2
O

On the other hand, the ·OH quantities produced from Cl-CDs and O-CDs were estimated by employing terephthalic acid (TA) as ·OH probe [12,13]. The experimental results support our above proposal that Cl-CDs can generate more ·OH radicals than O-CDs (Fig. S5 in Supplementary file). Therefore, this work has opened a new way to tune the photochemical properties of CDs through heteroatoms although further works may be required.

Cl-CDs were synthesized through the substitution reaction between Cl radicals into thionyl chloride molecules and OH/COOH groups at the surface of O-CDs. The XPS results show that Cl atomic content in Cl-CDs with the size of 2–5nm is 2–3%. However, such Cl-CDs exhibit much higher photodegradation of Pc molecules than O-CDs and N-CDs. It is believed that the Cl-contained groups at the surface of CDs play a key role in driving the photochemical reaction.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: We thank for financial support from the National Natural Science Foundation of China (nos. 51272301, 51172214, and 51172120), China Postdoctoral Science Foundation funded project (nos. 2012M510788 and 2013T60269), Shanxi Province Science Foundation for Youths (2014021008), 131 Talent Plan of Higher Learning Institutions of Shanxi.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at http://dx.doi.org/10.1016/j.apsusc.2015.07.125.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following are the supplementary data to this article:

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Modulation and effects of surface groups on photoluminescence and photocatalytic activity of carbon dots

Hydrothermal treatment of grass: a low-cost, green route to nitrogen-doped, carbon-rich, photoluminescent polymer nanodots as an effective fluorescent sensing platform for label-free detection of Cu(II) ions
Carbon quantum dots and their applications
Semiconductor-mediated photodegradation of pollutants under visible-light irradiation
Carbon nanoparticles as visible-light photocatalysts for efficient CO2 conversion and beyond
Tailoring surface groups of carbon quantum dots to improve photoluminescence behaviors
Optically tunable amino-functionalized grapheme quantum dots
Covalent and noncovalent phthalocyanine–carbon nanostructure systems: synthesis, photoinduced electron transfer, and application to molecular photovoltaics
Atomically precise edge chlorination of nanographenes and its application in grapheme nanoribbons
Band bending in semiconductors: chemical and physical consequences at surface and interfaces
Properties of O2·− and OH· formed in TiO2 aqueous suspensions by photocatalytic reaction and the influence of H2O2 and some ions
Dosimetry in sonochemistry: the use of aqueous terephythalate ion as a fluorescence monitor
